_schema.purpose: Registry of City Auditor reports. Each entry documents what the auditor found, what action was warranted, and what the council actually did. The gap between warranted action and actual response is the scored event — not the audit release itself.

status_values.pending_council_action: Audit released; not yet presented to full council
status_values.received_filed: Council voted to receive and file; no substantive follow-up motion
status_values.substantive_response: At least one member moved for action addressing a finding
status_values.response_documented: Council response complete and follow-up pattern visible

fields.audit_ref: Unique key — used in incidents.json audit_ref field to link incidents to this entry
fields.title: Official report title
fields.key_findings: What the auditor documented — the ground truth
fields.warranted_action: What a taxpayer-aligned council should do in response
fields.council_agenda_date: Date the report was formally presented to council (null if not yet scheduled)
fields.council_response: What the council actually did at that meeting
fields.followup_pattern: Subsequent actions that reveal how the council internalized (or ignored) the findings
fields.scoring_note: The pattern the audit-response cycle reveals — what ordinary voters should understand
fields.source_extract: Path to structured extract file in sources/audits/ containing verbatim quotes, page references, and detailed key facts

homeless_response_team_2025.title: Berkeley Homeless Response Team: Coordination, Oversight, and Outcome Tracking
homeless_response_team_2025.author: City Auditor
key_findings: HRT coordination was paused for a significant period during the audit window
No outcome tracking system in place — the city cannot measure whether programs are working
Fragmentation between city and county homeless services creates duplication and coverage gaps
Berkeley operates standalone infrastructure parallel to Alameda County services with no consolidation analysis
homeless_response_team_2025.warranted_action: Direct staff to implement outcome tracking; commission a consolidation study with Alameda County; require performance benchmarks before renewing program contracts
homeless_response_team_2025.followup_pattern: No council member has publicly requested outcome metrics or proposed consolidation with county services as of April 2026
homeless_response_team_2025.scoring_note: Findings are primarily staff-operational — not directly attributable to individual council members. Becomes individually scored when/if a member is asked about outcomes and claims ignorance, or when program contracts are renewed without performance metrics being required.

streets_rocky_road_2025.title: Rocky Road: Berkeley Streets at Risk and Significantly Underfunded
streets_rocky_road_2025.author: City Auditor
key_findings: Pavement Condition Index (PCI) of 57 — rated 'at risk' by MTC, 10 points below regional average and near the threshold where deterioration accelerates rapidly
$42M/year required to achieve PCI 70; general fund allocates approximately $15M/year
Structural pattern: council has relied on a cycle of bond measures rather than general fund reprioritization
Five Year Paving Plan selects streets based on political considerations rather than efficiency-maximizing pavement management metrics
Bond dependence crowds out pressure to reprioritize the general fund — each bond cycle defers the underlying resource allocation question
recommendations: Reform Five Year Paving Plan selection criteria to prioritize by cost-efficiency metrics
Increase general fund allocation to streets before pursuing additional bond measures
Address the structural underfunding problem in the operating budget, not solely through debt
streets_rocky_road_2025.followup_pattern: 2026-03-17: council unanimously directed staff to develop a $300M infrastructure bond for the November 2026 ballot — the fifth bond cycle addressing streets since 2012. No companion motion to reform the Five Year Paving Plan, increase general fund allocation, or address any of the audit's structural findings. The audit's root cause diagnosis (GF underfunding, not lack of bond authority) was received, filed, and converted into a bond campaign.
streets_rocky_road_2025.scoring_note: The audit established that Berkeley's street problem is a spending prioritization failure, not a revenue shortage. The council's response — a fifth bond cycle — is the opposite of what the audit warranted. Members present on 2025-10-28 who later voted or advocated for the bond without a companion reprioritization motion acted with documented knowledge of the root cause.

financial_condition_2026.title: Berkeley's Financial Condition (FY2016–FY2025): Structural Deficit Poses Risk to Financial Sustainability
financial_condition_2026.author: City Auditor
key_findings: General Fund structural deficit of $32M (FY2027) and $33M (FY2028) — not a one-time gap
Recent budgets balanced using one-time measures: $4.7M from workers' comp reserve (FY2025), $3M withdrawal from Section 115 pension pre-funding trust
GF expenses grew 33% in real terms over the audit period; largest growth: community development/housing +119%, health/welfare +107%
FTE count grew 30% (1,336 to 1,747); salaries and benefits are approximately two-thirds of the GF budget
Net pension liability of $694.8M; funded ratio 66% (high risk, tied 2nd-worst among comparable jurisdictions)
Annual pension payments rising from $76M (FY2024) to projected $108M by FY2034
Section 115 pension pre-funding trust: met annual contribution goal in only 2 of 7 years; balance $29.5M after FY2025 withdrawal
Unfunded capital and deferred maintenance grew from $603.5M (FY2017) to $1.8B (FY2024)
Required biennial unfunded capital liability report for FY2025 not produced — staff cited 'limited staff time'
Council's adopted fiscal policies do not require assessing whether recurring revenues match recurring expenditures — a foundational GFOA best practice gap
Proposed $300M GO bond would increase debt service tax rates for approximately 20 years before declining
recommendations: Adopt GFOA best practice requiring assessment of recurring revenue/expenditure balance
Develop a plan to address the structural deficit using recurring measures, not one-time reserves
Restore Section 115 Trust to adequate funding levels and meet annual contribution goals
Produce the overdue biennial unfunded capital liability report
Extend Capital Improvement Plan horizon (currently 5 years, low end of GFOA recommendation)
financial_condition_2026.warranted_action: Any fiscally responsible council should: (1) adopt the GFOA balance requirement as a formal policy, (2) direct staff to identify recurring expenditure reductions to close the structural gap, (3) pause or condition bond discussions on demonstrating that operating baseline is addressed, (4) require the overdue capital liability report before voting on any capital bond measure
financial_condition_2026.followup_pattern: Prior to audit release: council unanimously directed staff (2026-03-17) to develop a $300M infrastructure bond and commission a poll on a half-cent sales tax — both revenue measures that do not address the structural operating deficit. This direction was given with knowledge of the deficit trajectory from prior budget documents.
financial_condition_2026.scoring_note: This is the highest-priority audit in the registry. The structural deficit, pension risk, and capital collapse are individually serious; together they describe a city whose fiscal trajectory is unsustainable. The council's revealed pre-audit response (bond + sales tax) addresses neither the recurring imbalance nor the pension underfunding. When this audit goes before the council, any member who moves to receive and file without a companion motion for structural reform is making a choice that this audit documents precisely.
